title: Chapter 20: The Offer
content: The Patriarch made his move at breakfast.

Not directly—Liang Tianming never moved directly. He used intermediaries, proxies, the weight of family obligation pressing down like gravity until you didn't realize you were falling until you hit the ground.
It started with Feng.

Ren found the younger man in the garden, sitting on the bench by the koi pond—the same spot where Ren and Xue had their pivotal conversations, now occupied by a brother who was slowly being eaten from the inside.
"Ren." Feng's voice was hollow. When he looked up, his eyes were brown and present—the entity dormant, the real Feng fighting for every moment of lucidity. "My father told me what you are."
"What did he tell you?"
"That you're a soldier from the future. That you've been manipulating my sister. That you want to destroy our family's legacy." A beat. "That you can cure me."
The last three words carried the weight of a drowning man's hope.
Ren sat beside him. The morning sun was warm, the koi colorful beneath the surface, the garden absurdly peaceful for a conversation that could determine the fate of billions.
"I can try. If I get enough power and the right access, I can force the parasite into dormancy and purge it from your system. But it won't be easy, and it won't be painless."
"My father says he can control it. That the supplements he gives me keep the infection in check. That if I follow his program, I'll be... enhanced. Stronger. Better."

"That's what the entity wants you to think. The supplements don't control the infection—they *feed* it. Each dose accelerates the spore's integration with your nervous system. Stage 3 is months away, Feng. After that, you're not *you* anymore. You're a puppet wearing your face."
Feng's hands clenched on his knees. "And if I believe you—what then? I betray my father? Turn against my own family?"

"Your family is building a machine that will end the world. Your father is *counting* on your infection to use you as fuel for the portal's activation. The spore inside you isn't a gift—it's a load-bearing component of the apocalypse." Ren gripped Feng's shoulder—not gently, but with the urgent certainty of someone who'd watched this exact scenario play out before. "I'm not asking you to betray your family. I'm asking you to *save* it. From itself."
Feng stared at him. The war behind his eyes was visible—the entity pulling one way, loyalty pulling another, self-preservation somewhere in between.
"If I help you," Feng said slowly, "I need something in return."
"Name it."
"When you cure me—*if* you cure me—I want to see my father face what he's done. Not revenge. Not punishment. Just... the truth. I want him to understand what he almost cost us."
"You have my word."
The younger man nodded once. Then he stood, walked back toward the estate, and didn't look back.
That afternoon, the Patriarch sent a formal invitation: dinner in the private dining room, family only, no guests. The subtext was clear—Xue, Feng, and the Patriarch. No Ren.
Xue showed Ren the invitation with a tight expression. "He's trying to separate us."
"I know. Go."
"What?"

"Go to dinner. Hear what he has to say. Let him think he's getting through to you." Ren took the invitation from her hands. "I need to know his pitch. The specifics matter—what he offers, what he threatens, where his red lines are."
"You want me to *spy* on my own father?"
"I want you to *survive* your own father. Which means understanding him better than he understands you."

Xue took a long breath. "He'll offer me Feng's life. He'll say if I cooperate with the portal project, he'll slow the spore infection—keep Feng alive, maintain his personality, stop the conversion. And if I refuse..."
"He'll let the spore take Feng and use him as the activation sacrifice."
"Yes." Her voice cracked. "He'll use the fear of losing my brother to make me complicit in losing everything else."

"That's why you need to hear the offer. Not to accept it—to *document* it. The specifics, the terms, the exact words he uses. That's ammunition, Xue. When we need to turn the remaining family members against him, the recording of Liang Tianming offering his own son's life as a bargaining chip will be more powerful than any army."
Xue looked at him with something Ren hadn't seen in her eyes before—not trust, not affection, but something deeper. Respect.
"You really were a war general, weren't you?"
"I was the last one standing. Which means I was also the worst one—because I didn't save anyone."

"That's not true." Her hand touched his cheek—a gesture so unexpected that Ren's enhanced senses flagged her pulse spiking, her pupils dilating, a cascade of involuntary responses that the system categorized as *emotional* rather than *strategic*. "You saved *me*. Before I even knew I needed saving."
Then she was gone, walking toward the private dining room with her spine straight and her armor back in place.
Xue reported back at midnight.
She came through the window again—the ledge, the dark, the impossible climb that was becoming routine. Her eyes were red-rimmed, her composure held together by willpower and spite.

"He offered exactly what you predicted. Feng's life in exchange for my cooperation. Specifically: I channel Node 4's containment frequency in reverse to accelerate the conversion, and he'll maintain Feng at Stage 2 indefinitely—never progressing, never recovered, but *himself*. Alive and aware."
"That's a lie."

"I know. The spore doesn't work that way. There's no 'holding at Stage 2'—the infection is progressive, and the supplements are accelerating it. He'd keep Feng stable just long enough to use him as the activation sacrifice, and then—" She shook her head. "But he said it with such *conviction*. Like he genuinely believed his own offer."
"True believers always do. Your father doesn't think he's lying. He thinks he's offering you a reasonable deal—you sacrifice one node, you save your brother. In his framework, it's a good trade."
"It's not a trade. It's extortion."
"Same thing, to a man like him." Ren pulled a small device from his pocket—a modified recording unit that Liu Haoran had supplied, shielded against array detection. "Did you bring the recording?"

Xue withdrew a jade hairpin from her hair. Embedded in the pin was a microscopic array recorder—the kind of thing that Qian Delun had pioneered, using dimensional frequency to capture audio without electronic components.
"Everything." She handed him the pin. "Every word. Every threat. Every lie."

Ren interfaced the pin with the Salvation System. The recording unfolded in his enhanced hearing: the Patriarch's smooth voice, the quiet confidence, the offer presented as generosity. And then, when Xue had asked what would happen if she refused, the shift in tone—the removal of the mask, the revelation of the predator beneath.

*"If you refuse, Xue, I won't force you. I'm your father, not your captor. But you should understand the consequences of your choice. Without my intervention, Feng's infection will progress to Stage 5 within fourteen months. At Stage 5, he won't be Feng anymore—he'll be a vessel, empty of everything except the entity's will. And the activation requires a bloodline sacrifice. Would you rather he go to the altar as your brother, aware and willing, or as a hollow shell that doesn't know its own name?"*

The words were chilling—calculated to wound, to terrify, to force a choice between two horrors. A father using his own son's degeneration as leverage against a daughter who loved that son more than she feared her father.
"He's a monster," Xue whispered.
"He's a strategist who's been playing this game for forty years," Ren said. "And he just made his first mistake."
"What mistake?"

"He told you his timeline. Fourteen months until Stage 5. That means I have fourteen months to reach 3% power, gain access to the cleansing arrays, and cure Feng." Ren pocketed the recording. "Your father thinks he's pressuring you. What he's actually doing is giving us a deadline we can hit."
The Salvation System confirmed:

`TIMELINE ANALYSIS:`
`Feng's Stage 5 threshold: 14 months`
`Current War God power: 1.3%`
`Power growth rate: Accelerating (containment feedback loop)`
`Projected power at 12 months: 3.7%`
`Cleansing array access: Requires Patriarch's Seal (Node 2) or Xue's full channeling capability`
`ASSESSMENT: Cure is achievable within the 14-month window IF:`
`1. Power growth continues at current or improved rate`
`2. Xue reaches advanced channeling capability`
`3. The Patriarch doesn't escalate to direct action before the window closes`
"So we have a year," Xue said.

"We have a year. And a recording that could turn every remaining family member against the Patriarch when the time is right." Ren took her hand—the gesture becoming automatic between them, a shared anchor in the storm. "We also have a traitor."

Feng. The young man who'd just pledged his loyalty in the garden—who could provide intelligence from inside the Patriarch's inner circle, who had bloodline access to areas Ren couldn't reach, who was fighting a war inside his own body and choosing Ren's side.
"Feng is with us," Xue said, reading his thoughts. "He's my brother. And he's the one with the most to lose."
"Then we have a year, a team, and a plan." Ren allowed himself a thin smile. "Not bad for a useless son-in-law."
Xue laughed—a real laugh, short and sharp, cutting through the midnight gloom like a blade of light.
"Definitely not useless." She squeezed his hand once, then slipped back through the window into the dark.
One thousand seventy days.
